serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup (approximately 250 ml / 250 g) of honey green tea as typically served at a Singaporean beverage stall or café.

nutrient_source.title: Honey Green Tea (No Topping) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Honey Green Tea (No Topping), cr_id D08010050. All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for honey-sweetened green tea. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on: (1) honey GI of approximately 55–58 per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); (2) brewed green tea itself has negligible GI contribution; (3) the sugar content of this beverage (7.49g/100g) is predominantly from honey, which has a well-established low-to-medium GI. Glycemic load per 250g serving = (55 × 7.49 × 2.5) / 100 = 10.3g. This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended for clinical use.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.49g sugar per 100g exceeds the 5g/100g SSB threshold; added sugars independently elevate blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines regardless of the low sodium content (1mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this drink in its standard form and request plain unsweetened green tea, as the honey-derived sugar content (7.49g/100g) independently raises cardiovascular and blood pressure risk even at low sodium levels.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.49g free sugars per 100g and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugars are absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial blood glucose elevation, which is particularly hazardous for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid honey green tea in this sweetened form entirely and substitute plain unsweetened green tea, as the 7.49g/100g free sugar content with no fibre buffer triggers rapid glycaemic excursions incompatible with blood glucose management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages (7.49g sugar/100g) is associated with elevated triglycerides, increased visceral adiposity, and heightened CVD risk; green tea polyphenols offer partial mitigation but do not offset the sugar burden in this sweetened form.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit or avoid this sweetened beverage and opt for plain unsweetened green tea to benefit from EGCG polyphenols without the added sugar load that raises triglycerides and CVD risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: 7.49g added sugar per 100g with zero dietary fibre promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; the anti-inflammatory benefit of green tea EGCG is negated by the honey sugar content in this formulation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should switch to plain unsweetened green tea to obtain EGCG anti-inflammatory benefits without the pro-inflammatory effect of 7.49g/100g added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honey Green Tea (No Topping) is a freshly brewed green tea beverage sweetened with honey, served without any customisable toppings such as pearls, jelly, or pudding. It is a popular ready-to-drink speciality tea widely available at Singaporean beverage stalls, bubble tea shops, and cafés. The drink is prepared by steeping green tea leaves or powder in hot water and blending with honey to achieve a lightly sweet, floral, and mildly astringent flavour profile. The absence of toppings results in a clean, liquid-only beverage with a smooth texture. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 29 kcal, 7.49g carbohydrate (entirely as sugar from honey), 0.01g protein, 0g fat, 0g dietary fibre, and 1mg sodium. The sugar content of 7.49g/100g classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under Singapore HPB and WHO definitions. Despite the presence of green tea polyphenols (catechins, EGCG), the added honey sugar negates anti-inflammatory and glycaemic benefits, making this drink unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis in its sweetened form. The glycaemic index is estimated at 55 (low) based on the honey component. A standard serving of 250ml delivers approximately 72.5 kcal and 18.7g sugar. Plain unsweetened green tea is recommended as a healthier alternative for clinical populations.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for cr_id D08010050 with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros are exact matches from the primary approved regional source. GI is an analogous estimate from a peer-reviewed source (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) and is clearly documented as such.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (D08010050); this is consistent with a brewed tea beverage containing no fat-bearing ingredients. saturated_fat_g and cholesterol_mg are confirmed zero by formulation (water, green tea, honey contain no saturated fat or cholesterol). dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source; brewed tea infusion does not retain fibre from tea leaves. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this specific honey green tea preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D08010050 as of 2026. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on honey GI (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); direct GI measurement for this beverage is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database. Ontology codes use approximate matches as no exact honey-sweetened green tea entries exist in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of 2026.
